items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: What does voltage (potential difference) across a component represent?
items.correct: The energy transferred per unit charge passing through that component.
items.misconception: Voltage is the 'amount of electricity' flowing through the wire.
items.random_wrong: Voltage measures how many electrons are stored in a battery.
items.why: Students conflate voltage with current because both are 'something to do with electricity'; they don't distinguish 'energy per charge' from 'rate of charge flow'.

items.q: Which equation links power, current and voltage?
items.why: It's P = I × V. “P = V ÷ I” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 2 kW heater for 1 hour. “100 W bulb for 1 hour” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A toaster draws 4 A from a 230 V supply. What is its power, in W?
items.why: Exact answer: 920. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
